card.intuition: Replacing real parts of your system (like an API or a third-party library) with 'fake' versions that behave in a predictable way during tests.
card.analogy: A crash test dummy: you don't use a real person (the real API) to test a car crash (the test). You use a dummy (the Mock) that looks and acts enough like a person to give you the data you need without the risk or cost of a real human.
when_to_use: Testing components that fetch data from an API
Simulating error responses (404, 500) from the server
Isolating a component from its complex parent dependencies
gotchas: Over-mocking can lead to 'False Positives': your tests pass because your mocks are too simple, even though the real app would break
Always prefer mocking the 'Network' (using MSW) rather than mocking the 'code' (using jest.mock), as it tests more of your actual app logic

exercise.prompt: Identify the popular library used to mock network requests at the browser/service-worker level.
exercise.starter_code:
def identify_tool():
    # It stands for 'Mock Service Worker'
    tool = "___"
    return tool
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.upper() == "MSW"
assert check("MSW")
print("PASS")
